title: The progressive executive power delusion
gist: Yglesias argues Democrats are wrong to assume they can wield expansive executive power the way Trump has if they retake the White House, since the Supreme Court's current doctrines aren't reliably on their side, and warns against progressive branding that reflexively mirrors Trump's playbook.

themes.theme: The limits of a future Democratic executive power strategy

insights.claim: Yglesias's central thesis is that progressives who assume a future Democratic president could replicate Trump's expansive use of executive power are mistaken, because the current Supreme Court's legal doctrines are not reliably favorable to left-wing uses of that power.
insights.speaker: Matthew Yglesias
insights.so_what: A caution against assuming legal precedent set by Trump-era executive actions will transfer to a future Democratic administration; relevant to handicapping the durability of any future left-of-center unilateral policy moves.
insights.quote: The Supreme Court isn't on your side.

insights.claim: Trump's use of executive power has produced mixed legal outcomes — some initiatives have failed in court while others have scored important victories — meaning Democrats should study both his successes and current legal doctrine before planning how to govern if they retake power in 2029 or 2033.
insights.speaker: Matthew Yglesias
insights.so_what: Suggests a research agenda for anyone modeling future executive-action risk/opportunity: track which specific Trump executive actions survived court challenges versus which were struck down.
insights.locator: Donald Trump has wielded executive power in creative and aggressive ways

insights.claim: Yglesias criticizes a Democratic planning effort branded 'Project 2029' as a 'perverse' example of Democrats mirror-imaging Trump-style politics, noting the irony that it echoes branding (implicitly evoking Project 2025) that Trump himself had to publicly disavow as a liability.
insights.speaker: Matthew Yglesias
insights.so_what: Flags a specific naming/branding misstep as a symptom of a deeper strategic error: Democrats copying the form of Trumpism without weighing whether the underlying approach actually worked.
insights.locator: I've noticed a perverse tendency for Democrats to mirror-image Trump

insights.claim: A photo accompanying the piece shows advocates demanding Joe Biden cancel student debt, used as an example of the kind of unilateral executive action progressives have wanted Democratic presidents to take.
insights.speaker: Matthew Yglesias
insights.so_what: Grounds the piece's critique in a concrete recent example of progressive demands for unilateral executive action.
